-- 2 of 40 --

TRANSCRIPT:
SCRIPT BUILDER TECHNOLOGY
The following is a transcript of the content on your DVD and CD (adapted
for readability):
Introduction
Hi, this is Jordan Belfort and I want to welcome you to the Script Building
Technology Section of the Straight Line System. And I think of all the
different sections of the Straight Line Technology, this is perhaps in its
own way, the most important because what a script really does is, %&;/1&<"1
="''1&<+35<&1+3&1"//"$0"1+)1&<"14"*)"0&1/-'":11
In other words, it allows you essentially to go to all the past experiences
you have with selling a product and to give you all your best lines, in
exactly the right order. It basically allows you to keep complete control of
the sale, and to sound like you know what you’re talking about it. It allows
you to answer objections in a way that it doesn’t take you off the Straight
J'(.#-(0#"))#'(&"#K/8&"#:%-(8+2#%'1*&V##<&#;..$+#9"8#'(#&*-&#3",)"%&#C"(.#
where you’re in control of the sale.
From a company’s perspective, meaning management, it allows you to
+9+&.,'C.#9"8%#+-/.+#)"%3.#-(0#,-(-1.#'&#-//#'(#&*.#+-,.#)-+*'"(6##>*'+#
is crucial to a company’s uniformity and really allows you to grow and
.F$-(0#-(0#08$/'3-&.#&*'(1+#'(#-#=-9#&*-&#')#9"8#=-/;#'(&"#"(.#")43.2#-(0#
'(&"#-("&*.%#")43.2#"%#-("&*.%#")43.2#.5.%9"(.@+#0"'(1#&*'(1+#&*.#+-,.2#
precise way. That’s important not just for sales, but for results.
It’s also important for ethical and regulatory reasons, because in the
absence of scripts, what I’ve found very often is that sales people almost
JORDAN BELFORT
Video Transcript	2

-- 3 of 40 --

always start going off the Straight Line and saying things sometimes
that are really, really stupid. 	And sometimes even things that are really,
really not true. Sometimes salesmen do that no doubt and they’re bad
intentioned. But I would say more often than not, perhaps 90% or better
of the time, it’s not so much that the salesman wants to lie or be deceitful.
They just run out of intelligent things to say. And once they run out of
intelligent things to say, they start making stuff up. They think it sounds
good as they’re saying it. But when actually, on the other end listening to
it, it’s hard on the ears.
So, what it really does, is it gives you the opportunity to really go through
an entire sale from A to Z and script out all of the rebuttals. So you really
;("=#-//#&*.#"7X.3&'"(+#7.)"%.*-(06##M.3-8+.#/.&@+#+-9#&*.%.#-%.#45.#"%#
six different rebuttals that people ever come up with—this allows you to
create an airtight logical AND an airtight emotional case to overcome
people’s objections and create those Three Ten’s I speak about [in the
Straight Line Persuasion system]:
1) 	The Product.
2) 	Yourself.
3) 	And Your Company.
It also allows you to make those airtight logical and emotional cases
without lying, exaggerating, or wasting your prospect’s time.
Finally, it allows you to make effective use of tonality and body language.
Remember, only 9% of human communication is the actual words.
A lot more of it is how you say your words—the tone in which you say
them—and how you move your body when you’re face to face. What it
chunks up to at higher levels, is that you’re a person worth listening to
and that you’re a person who can help them achieve their goals and get
their own outcomes.
JORDAN BELFORT
Video Transcript	3

-- 4 of 40 --

Let’s say you have a tonality of absolute certainty. I say, “Let me tell you
something. You do NOT understand how incredible this product is.” Now,
those words themselves are stupid. But let’s just say it this way: “I can’t
tell you, how incredible this product is.” 	No I say, “I can’t tell you how
incredible this product is.” (said in a softer, more certain tone). 	Which
one sounds more compelling? Now, of course, the words are kind of
ridiculous. But, you saw that when I applied a tonality of certainty to the
same set of words, it took on a much greater meaning and it starts to
move you emotionally.
Another thing I want you to notice is that when I started talking about the
scripting, I was very certain. I started sounding certain. Notice how I
started moving my hands. I started smacking the back of my right hand
into my left palm. That’s a gesture that I use to create certainty.
Now, it happens automatically but for your purposes, as you’re learning to
muster that certain tone whenever you want it, you’ll notice that you have
a gesture that you’ll do automatically when you want to summon the tone
of certainty.
So once you identify that gesture, which, mine is this (Jordan shows
the gesture again), if you need to sound certain and you know there’s a
certain spot in your presentation, and you want to be absolutely certain
about, you can create that tone and assist yourself in creating a tone by
using the gesture. So I’d say, “Watch. This is the most incredible product
that we’ve ever released.” (Jordan says this while doing gesture at the
same time.) Again, you saw much more power with the latter versus the
former. You notice how I automatically bang my hand into the back of my
hand? And for me, so you know, that’s my gesture. You might have an
entirely different gesture.
So, what I’m going to ask you to do, and this is another element of
the Straight Line System, is learn how to link certain gestures to your
JORDAN BELFORT
Video Transcript	4

-- 5 of 40 --

tonalities. But for the purposes of script building, remember, you’re going
to see, once you have a great script that has a complete (1) Introduction,
(2) a Body and (3) a Close, you’re going to see that there are certain
+$.3'43#+$"&+#9"8#(..0#&"#+"8(0#5.%9#3.%&-'(6##N&*.%#+$"&+#=*.%.#9"8#
need to have a tone of sincerity, or a tone of reasonableness. Whatever
those different tones are based on your outcome. We’ll go through all
those tones in a moment. But what you’ll see is, that once you have a
script, you know where they are. You can use them and summon them at
the precise moment.
The Power of Straight Line Scripts
Now, the question really is, “Why my scripts? What are really special
about the scripts I write? And you know, are they better than other
scripts? Are they different?” And the answer is, “Well, I think you’re a
hell of a lot better than other scripts out there.” But most important,
they’re structured in a way that’s formulaic. In other words, they can be
duplicated for any industry. For any outcome that you want. And I want
to give you some very basic structure here. Some things that allow you,
really, to adapt your industry to the Straight Line System.
Let me just digress for a second to Hollywood. Because there’s The Wolf
of Wall Street movie in production with Leonardo DiCaprio, right? And the
script for that movie, when you read the script, because it’s a phenomenal
script, it doesn’t sound like the actors are reading from a script. In other
words, the lines are written in such a way that it sounds natural. And it’s
pleasing to the ear. That’s an important quality to any script. See, you
can never sound like you’re reading from a script. You have to learn to
master the art and again, it’s a Straight Line Technology to learn to master
the art of reading a script without sounding like you’re reading a script.
E(0#&*-&#3-(#7.#'(#$.%+"(#"%#'(D8.(3'(1#)-3.#&"#)-3.#Y7.3-8+.#")#3"8%+.#
you don’t have a script in front of you when you’re selling face to face),
JORDAN BELFORT
Video Transcript	5

-- 6 of 40 --

and when you’re on the phone (where yes, you can have one). But either
way, the most important thing is that you sound natural in the words that
you say, the syllables, the number of beats, and the rhythm is pleasing to
the ear. So that’s one thing that’s very, very important in my scripts.
Another thing is the scripts that I write are not what’s called “frontloaded.”
E(0#=*-&#<#,.-(#79#&*-&#'+#&*-&#9"8#0"(@&#$8&#.5.%9#+'(1/.#7.(.4&#-(0#
every single feature right up front where you try and blast the person
and try to get them to these super high levels of certainty right away.
What happens is, typically the way we’re built as human beings, is that
9"8#3-(@&#1.&#)%",#C.%"#&"#S^^#/';.#&*-&6##>*.%.#*-5.#&"#7.#&*.+.#/'&&/.#
stopping off points. For example, you get someone who is maybe not
that certain about your product or you. And you run a language pattern.
You go through part of a script and they become more certain. And then
you back off and then you can again reapproach them again. Make
them even more certain. You back off again. A little bit later in the
conversation, you make them even more certain.
In other words, you can raise someone’s level of certainty up, bit by bit.
You can’t get it all at once. So what happens is, very often, the mistake
I see in poorly written scripts with people who don’t know the Straight
Line Technique, is they frontload. They put all the great stuff up front.
Then when the person inevitably says, “Let me think about it,” they have
("&*'(1#./+.#&"#+-9#&"#3"(5'(3.#&*-&#$.%+"(#&"#'(D8.(3.#&*-&#$.%+"(#&*-&#
NOW is the best time to make the decision. So, it’s very important not to
front load.
And also, another thing about the scripts that I write as part of the Straight
Line System, is that each script is an integral part to an overall System.
<&@+#("&#&*-&#&*.%.@+#"(.#+3%'$&6##>*.%.#,'1*&#7.#45.#"%#+'F#0')).%.(&#+3%'$&+#
that you go through from the entire process, from A to Z. From Open
to Close. (1) You have your main qualifying script. (2) You have your
.++.(&'-/#M"09#")#9"8%#$%.+.(&-&'"(6##YiT#E(0#4(-//92#9"8#*-5.#O.78&&-/#
JORDAN BELFORT

-- 7 of 40 --

+3%'$&+6##E(0#&*.#'0.-#'+2#9"8#(..0#&"#*-5.#)"8%#"%#45.#0')).%.(&#5.%9#
cohesive statements. I want to call them wraps, so to speak, that you can
make about your product. About yourself. And each statement that you
make is designed to raise up someone’s level of certainty about the Three
Tens. And at the same time, to move them down the Straight Line from
the Open to the Close.
I’m just drawing on the board, just so you can see here. So just so, I’ll
run through what I’m talking about. Remember, visually, the Straight Line
(Jordan draws the Straight Line on the board). So what happens is, while
your main script might take you from here to here. Let’s say your here
to here is your qualifying script OP)#548'!)18&2'&)'&0"'(#2&'QLF')$'&0"'718"M,
Here’s your Main Script (Jordan points to the middle of the line). And
here’s the Script where you’re dealing with rebuttals and objections, here
(Jordan points to the last 1/3 of the line). Each of these scripts is integral
to the Straight Line.
If at any point in time, you do one of these things it will take you off the
Straight Line:
1) 	Break rapport with the prospect.
2) 	Say something that’s just entirely ridiculous. Or it’s a lie and they
catch you in a lie.
3) 	B-9#&*'(1+#&*-&#&-;.#&*.#$.%+"(#"8&#")#&*.#-3&8-/#'(D8.(3'(1#
conversation itself.
Remember your boundaries here? Above and below the line. If at
any point, you don’t have your scripts and you start talking about
the price of tea in China, things that aren’t actually germane to the
'(D8.(3'(1#+'&8-&'"(2#9"8#/"+.6##R++.(&'-//92#9"8#/"+.#&*.#-7'/'&9#&"#3/"+.#
the deal.
JORDAN BELFORT

-- 8 of 40 --

So, scripts occur here, here and here (Jordan points to the three
sections along the straight line – beginning, middle, end). And every
one of them is equally important. And when used together, you
create that goal. which I laid out earlier, which is to close any one
who’s closeable. This doesn’t mean everyone, but you want to close
everyone who’s closeable.
Elements of Script Writing
Now let’s talk about the different various elements of script writing.
And number one of course, is basic structure for a script. There are
three main sections. Let me write them down here.
I. Introduction
It starts with your >$&*+!30&%+$:11
This is a short Introduction. If it’s a cold call, or someone that
you’re calling or you’re in a store situation where someone walks
in and it’s how you introduce yourself. Let’s go outbound here.
That we’re actually calling someone or walking in someone’s door
-(0#&%9'(1#&"#'(D8.(3.#&*.,6##B"#9"8#*-5.#9"8%#'(&%"083&'"(#4%+&6##
That’s where you essentially do three things:
a. 	You introduce yourself.
b. 	You tell them the reason why you’re there today.
c. 	Then you ask them for permission to qualify them. That’s
how an introduction, in the short order, that’s really how it’s
broken down to three sections.
Section A is where you introduce yourself with a greeting. Section
B is where you actually tell them the reason why you’re there
JORDAN BELFORT
Video Transcript	8

-- 9 of 40 --

today—the reason why you’re there. And C is, you ask for
permission to qualify.
And that’s your basic script for any sort of cold call. Whether it’s in
person, or over the telephone.
II. Main Body of your Presentation
From there, you transition into the ?-%$1@+!( of your script.
And typically, from your Intro to your Body, is that after you qualify
someone, you uncover their needs, desires, and pain. After you
have a pretty good understanding of that, you move into the Body,
which accomplishes three things:
a. 	You determine that your product RSTU'(77'&0"1#'8""5, If it
doesn’t, you should end the call and not try to close someone
who shouldn’t be closed.
b. 	You also understand that they are (848%1477*'9+471("5,''
Meaning, your product could meet all their needs, but they
may not have the money. And you know, it’s not fair to bat
someone over the head that doesn’t have money because you
just make them feel like crap when they know that you can
7.(.4&#&*.,2#9.&#&*.9@%.#+&83;#'(#&*.'%#7"F#7.3-8+.#")#,"(.96##
So, you don’t want to sit there banging your head against
the wall, trying to close people who don’t have money for
your products.
c. 	And, the next step would be your offer—your product, your
service, or your coaching program. Whatever it might be that
="8/0#4//#&*"+.#(..0+#-(0#*./$#&*.,#1.&#"8&#")#$-'(#-(0#'(&"#
pleasure. Your transition is a very simple line which is: “Based
JORDAN BELFORT

-- 10 of 40 --

)8'"6"#*&018<'*)+'V+2&'2415'&)'A"C'&012'!#)<#4A'12'4'!"#$"%&'(&'
for you.” Now, notice the difference there when I use tonality.
i. 	Here’s casual: “Well, based on everything you just
2415'&)'A"C'&012'12'5"(81&"7*'4'!"#$"%&'(&'$)#'*)+,-
That’s way number one.
ii. 	Way number two: “Well, let me say this. Based on
"6"#*&018<'*)+'V+2&'2415'&)'A"C'&012'12'5"(81&"7*'4'
!"#$"%&'(&'$)#'*)+,-
Which one has more power? The second. Operative word there
was 0.4('&./9. And I said it with the hard D. It was like, ?R"(81&"7*'
4'!"#$"%&'(&'$)#'*)+,- That again, is the tone of certainty and also
-#&"(.#")#.(&*8+'-+,2#-(0#-#&"(.#")#3"(40.(3.2#%'1*&V#E(02#9"8#
sound like what? An expert.
d. 	So, that’s your transition to your Main Body. Now, here’s what’s
most important for the Main Body:
i. 	Number one—that it’s not frontloaded, which
,.-(+#9"8#0"(@&#1"#'(#/'+&'(1#7.(.4&#-)&.%#7.(.4&#
-)&.%#7.(.4&#-)&.%#7.(.4&#-)&.%#7.(.4&#-(0#7.3",.#
an expert of your product and start talking about
all the features, and, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah,
blah. When you frontload, you end up preaching
about your product, and they’re just listening, but
eventually, they tune out.
ii. 	So, your script should be short. The body should
be no more than, let’s say a half to three quarters of
a page. And, when you’re done with it, you should
have given one or two very powerful distinctions
about your product. And remember, we don’t sell
).-&8%.+6##>*'+#'+#M-+'3#<(D8.(3.#S^S6##A.#0"(@&#
JORDAN BELFORT
Video Transcript	10

-- 11 of 40 --

sell features—we sell 7.(.4&+. People are not so
concerned about, you know, every feature that a
computer might have. What they WANT to know is:
“Will it make my life easier?” Will it make it easy
&"#+8%)#&*.#<(&.%(.&#&"#4(0#"8&#&*.#&*'(1+#&*.9#=-(&V##
Will it allow them to have more time to spend with
their family because they can be more productive?
You get it? So you don’t sell features. You
+.//#7.(.4&+6##
Now, in terms of the A+=1+)1-14*"/"$&-&%+$7#'&#(..0+#&"#D"=#
+.-,/.++/96##<(#"&*.%#="%0+2#'&#*-+#&"#D"=#'(#-#=-9#=*.%.#&*.%.#-%.#
certain chunks that sound great. But then there are, I call them
stopping off points, which are these sections where you have
transitions. So, if you say a powerful statement, and then you say
another powerful statement, and then you say another powerful
statement, eventually, the three powerful statements start to lose
their power.
So the idea is, after you say one powerful statement, you want to
hammer something home. You say to the client, “You follow me
so far?” And they say, “Yes.”
You get it? So you want to say, “You follow me so far?”
“Make sense?”
“Are you with me?”
Get the person to say, “Yes.”
JORDAN BELFORT
Video Transcript	11

-- 12 of 40 --

On two levels, that’s important:
1) 	Number one, so the person doesn’t tune out because you’re
just preaching to them versus making it a dialogue. You want
a dialogue that is back and forth, not a monologue.
2) 	Secondly, because you want to get the prospect into the habit
of saying, “Yes.” You don’t want to be asking questions that
might end in “No.” When the person is saying, “No, no, no, no,
no,” then when you want to get the “Yes” out of them for your
-3&8-/#3/"+.2#'+#-#7'&#,"%.#0')438/&6
Now, understand this. You know, my technique of Straight Line is
far more advanced than simply thinking that we can get people to
say “Yes”#45.#&',.+#&"#+',$/.#+&8))2#-(0#&*-&#&*-&#='//#-//"=#&*.,#&"#
close. It’s not like that. What I’m saying is, is, you DON’T want to
get somebody into the habit of saying “No.” Okay?
When you say, “Does that make sense so far?” and they say,
“Yeah, it does,” then, you’re in rapport.
If you say, “Does that make sense?,” and they say, “No. It
doesn’t,” if you try to go forward, you’re out of rapport. All they’re
saying is, “This guy doesn’t care about what I’m saying. He’s not
even listening to me.[##:(0.%+&-(0V##
So, these little stopping off points, where you actually say,
“You with me so far? Make sense?” and they say “Yes!” keep
you in rapport.
Next. The use of what I call colloquialisms and what these are.
What it really means is, /4"-8%$51%$1'-(B-$;/1&"*B/: You
don’t want to speak in a way that sounds like you’re using a
JORDAN BELFORT
Video Transcript	12

-- 13 of 40 --

canned script. You want to sound natural, like you’re speaking
to a friend—speaking to someone that you want to connect with
emotionally and not just logically.
Now, along with that, remember, you still have to sound like an
expert. So there’s a balance to be struck. It’s not all like: “Hey,
buh, buh, buh. We are like, these, them, dos and don’ts”—like a
Brooklyn guy who’s not educated, right? You still want to sound
educated, like an expert. But, don’t try to talk above somebody or
8+.#-//#&*.+.#/'&&/.#78CC#$*%-+.+6##
This will make the person tune out. So, use colloquialisms, and
make it sound natural. But, always remember that when you’re
'(D8.(3'(12#9"8#(..0#&"#7.#$.%3.'5.0#-+#&*.#.F$.%&6####
Next point here with scripts. The best way to master a script is
to actually 4*-0&%0"1.(1*"-!%$51&<"1/0*%4&1+3&1'+3!:11And the
way I do this, especially in the beginning when I’m developing a
+3%'$&#'+2#<@//#=%'&.#&*.#4%+&#1.(.%-&'"(#")#-#+3%'$&#-(0#<@//#-3&8-//9#1"#
out and start working with the sales force, and I’ll listen to them
and hear which words are giving them trouble. Which words are
&"(18.G&='+&.%+V##A*-&#+.(&.(3.+#-%.(@&#D"='(1#3"%%.3&/9V##A*-&#
transitions are awkward? Then, I rewrite the script and go back
and train them again until I make sure that every single word in
&*.#+3%'$&#D"=+#+.-,/.++/96##E(0#-(97"092#("&#X8+&#&*.#&"$2#&"$2#
salesmen there can say it well.
One of the key elements is what I call balance. In other words, the
script balances with the number of syllables, the number of beats
in each sentence. If something just goes and rambles on and
rambles on, the human ear is very attune to the repetition, and it
tunes out. So you have to make sure that it’s pleasing to the ear.
There’s a correct number of syllables.
JORDAN BELFORT
Video Transcript	13

-- 14 of 40 --

And remember that you’re always closing. Not necessarily the sale
itself, but you’re closing on that the person should want to listen to
the next paragraph or even to the next word that you say.
E(0#-1-'(2#'&#3",.+#0"=(#&"#&*.#4%+&#45.#="%0+#&*-&#9"8#+-96#<#
said earlier that as human beings, we judge books by their covers.
We were taught not to, but we all do. And, even the people who
taught us not to, judge books by their covers—like our parents,
right? It’s human nature to do that.
B"#%.-//92#'(#&*.#4%+&#)"8%#+.3"(0+2#&*.#$."$/.#=*",#9"8@%.#&%9'(1#
&"#'(D8.(3.#-%.#-/%.-09#,-;'(1#-#,.(&-/#$'3&8%.#")#9"86#>*.9#1.&#
to either judge you as being the expert, that enthusiastic, sharp
-+#-#&-3;#$.%+"(6##N%2#&*.9@%.#1"(1#&"#4(0#9"8#08//2#7"%'(12#-(0#
not enthusiastic, at which point they will say to themself, “Ah. This
person’s not worth listening to.”
B"2#'&@+#5.%9#',$"%&-(&#&*-&#&*"+.#4%+&#="%0+2#/';.#9"8#;("=2#=*.(#
you say to someone as simple as: “Hi. Is Pam there?” or “HI. Is
Pam there?”
Same words. But the second one comes out upbeat, enthusiastic,
and sharp as a tack. Now, of course, Pam is not going to buy
because I say, “HI, is Pam there?”
But if I say, “Hi, is Pam there?” She’s like, “Uch! Another dull
intrusion to my already dull day.” Versus like, “Ooh, well.”
If I say, “HI! Is Pam there?” She’s like, “Hmm. What’s that?”
When you’re enthusiastic, you sort of break somebody’s state, and
you can essentially elevate them to a higher state.
JORDAN BELFORT
Video Transcript	14

-- 15 of 40 --

N(#+",.#/.5./2#'(D8.(3.#'&+./)#'+#-7"8&#&*.#&%-(+).%.(3.#")#
emotion—the emotion of certainty. You’re going into an encounter
where you’re very certain about your product, service, or
coaching—whatever it is you’re doing. And, the person’s not
that certain. So, you’re transferring that emotion of certainty to
somebody else. So, it’s very important that you learn to master
those tonal patterns, and especially in the beginning, to come off
+*-%$#-+#-#&-3;2#.(&*8+'-+&'3#-+#*.//2#-(0#-+#-(#.F$.%&#'(#9"8%#4./06##
Next, with your scripts: it’s %B4+*&-$&1&<-&1(+3;*"1<+$"/&1-$!1
"&<%0-':11Okay? This is very easy to do because when you’re
writing the script, ask yourself with every single sentence, “Am
I coming from a place of integrity? Am I coming from a place of
ethics?” Or, “Am I starting to exaggerate and sort of mislead?”
I’ll tell you stories. I’ve written some scripts back in the early days
when I was discovering The Straight Line that were accurate,
but they weren’t ethical. They weren’t lies, but there were some
misstatements, or really omissions of fact.
So make sure that for your own integrity, you keep your scripts
accurate. Don’t include any lies or wild exaggerations. And by the
way, just so you know, if you’re a sales manager, owner, or an
entrepreneur looking to use the Script Building Technology, if you
put a script out into your sales force and it’s riddled with lies or
exaggerations, they know it.
And, if your sales people are out there hammering on the phone or
'(#&*.#4./0#.5.%9#0-92#-(0#&*.9@%.#&.//'(1#/'.+2#'&#+&-%&+#&"#+..$#'(&"#
your entire corporate culture. And, the next thing you know, your
people are trying to rob and steal from you. Your own employees
will steal from you because they think you’re a thief after you’ve
told them to go out and lie to people. So, this idea that you can
JORDAN BELFORT
Video Transcript	15

-- 16 of 40 --

have a script that’s exaggerated and full of deceit and that it’s not
going to permeate your entire corporate culture, is foolish.
So, the scripts need to be accurate. They need to be compliant.
E(02#&*.9#(..0#&"#%.-//9#%.D.3&#9"8%#3"%$"%-&.#38/&8%.2#=*'3*#'+#
one of ethics and integrity.
So the question I get asked is, CD<"$1/<+3'!1>13/"1B(1/0*%4&EF
And the answer is, ALWAYS. You should always use the script
whether in person or on the phone.
So you say, CD"''71<+=1!+1>13/"1&<"1/0*%4&1%)1>;B1%$14"*/+$EF
j.%9#+',$/.\9"8#*-5.#'&#,.,"%'C.06##?"8#=-(&#&"#;("=#&*.#
scripts so well that you move past the point of the actual words.
Remember, 9% of human communication is words. The other
fSk#'+#&"(-/'&9#-(0#7"09#/-(18-1.6##M9#,.,"%'C'(1#&*.#+3%'$&#
and knowing the words themselves, the rest of your brain can be
focusing on your tonality and body language.
You will also be able to really look at the person and try to gather
the by closely listening to them, rather then being so caught up in
your own head. In contrast, if you’re caught up in your own words
and trying to say them by heart, your conscious brain will be using
-//#'&@+#$%"3.++'(1#$"=.%#"(#&*-&6##E(02#9"8@//#4(0#9"8@//#7.#,'++'(1#
those vital cues that you can get from the other people.
As you start writing more and more scripts, you can pull various
language patterns from other scripts and make them into a
cohesive pattern. And to that end, I’m giving you some sample
scripts that you can actually model in this Script Building
Technology. And modeling is just going to save you a tremendous
amount of time to learn because, again, as you know, successes
come from trial and error.
JORDAN BELFORT
Video Transcript	16

-- 17 of 40 --

And, I have spent over 25 years writing scripts almost in a way
=*.%.#<@,#-#C.-/"&\<#&"%&8%.##,9+./)#"5.%#.5.%9#+'(1/.#="%02#.5.%9#
single paragraph, every single transition. So, learn from what I’ve
done. Model my scripts. Translate them—take them and use
them into your own industry.
Now, in terms of G-$53-5"1H-&&"*$/—we very often talk about
the use of effective Language Patterns. And really, what are
Language Patterns? And how do you use them to close the
deal? 	And essentially what is a Language Pattern? It’s a chunk
of words. N'!4#4<#4!0')#'&=)'!4#4<#4!02'&04&'042'4'5"(8"5'
3"<18818<'485'4'5"(8"5'"85'=1&0'4'2!"%1(%')+&%)A"'4&&4%0"5'
to it.
?"8#3-(#*-5.#-#J-(18-1.#K-&&.%(#&*-&@+#+$.3'43-//9#0.+'1(.0#&"#
increase the Certainty of your product. You can have a Language
Pattern that is designed to sell your company, one that’s designed
to sell yourself.
80 percent of what I do is corporate work, and only 20 percent is
seminars. So, when I go to a company and train the sales forces, I
='//#-3&8-//9#*-5.#&*.#+-/.+#$."$/.#="%;#-(0#3%.-&.#45.#J-(18-1.#
Patterns. Five chunks they can use that are intelligent, ethical,
$.%+8-+'5.2#-(0#+.F96##l'5.#-7"8&#&*.,+./5.+2#45.#-7"8&#&*.#
3",$-(9#&*-&#&*.9#="%;#)"%2#-(0#45.#&*-&#*./$#&*.,#+.//#
the product.
And remember again, we have I<"1J1I"$/: And that’s:
1) 	Product
2) 	You
3) 	Your Company
JORDAN BELFORT
Video Transcript	17

-- 18 of 40 --

And the Language Patterns for each are designed to get people
to absolute certainty. Our goal is to create a ten for all three of
these things. That’s what your Language Patterns are doing. And
they’re occurring all along the Straight Line.
So, in the Open, they might be at a three on your product, a two
on you, and a one on your company. And as they go down the
Straight Line, you keep increasing this and moving down the
continuum. Increasing, increasing until BOOM!—you get to a ten,
ten, ten.
And then, there are Language Patterns that will lower someone’s
action threshold, as well. And, there are Language Patterns
that help elevate the pain threshold. And, of course, I caution
that when you elevate somebody’s pain, do it in a way that’s
empathetic and sympathetic. You’re not there to make someone
*8%&\9"8@%.#&*.%.#&"#,-;.#+",."(.#%.-/'C.#&*-&#=*'/.#&*.9#,'1*&#
think that not taking action is in their best interest. However, in the
real world, where they sit right now, if they don’t take action, they
will be feeling a lot of pain, because they’re already feeling pain
right now. And, if they don’t make any decisions; if they don’t take
any new actions, well, nothing’s going to change.
But, it has to be balanced with giving them a solution as well. And,
that you’ll be the guy who can help them. You can get them to
where they need to be in life. You can move them closer to their
goals and their vision.
JORDAN BELFORT
Video Transcript	18

-- 19 of 40 --

III. The Close
And lastly, of course, you have I<"1K'+/":
And, this is the section where you actually ask for the order. Now,
I’ll tell you something very ironic here—when I train sales forces,
5.%9#")&.(#<#4(0#&*-&#&*.#+-/.+,.(#=*"#-%.#("&#0"'(1#=.//#0"(@&#
ask for the order very much or even at all. How many times
should you ask for the order? It’s not once. It might be three,
)"8%2#45.2#"%#+'F#&',.+6##<&@+#0"(.#'(#-#5.%9#./.1-(&#=-92#-(0#'&@+#
accompanied by a tone of reasonableness and sincerity. Not a,
you know, “Give me one shot. You won’t be sorry.” It will be like,
“Give me one shot, and believe me. You will not be sorry. Sound
fair enough?”##>*-&@+#,"%.#")#-#>"(-/#K-&&.%(#&*-&#9"8#="8/0#4(0#
in a Close.
The typical Language Pattern for a Close would be along the lines
of, “Give me one shot and believe me, if I’m even half right, the
only problem you’ll have is that I didn’t call you six months ago
and get you started then. Sound fair enough?”
Now, there are actually three tonal patterns in that close. I’ll tell
you what they are.
1) 	Number one is a tone of absolute certainty, which is, “Just give
me one shot, and believe me. If I’m even HALF right.” That’s
certainty.
2) 	Then you transition from certainty into sincerity, which would
be, “The only problem you’ll have is if they didn’t call you six
months ago.” Not, “The only problem you’ll have is I didn’t call
you.” That’s NOT sincerity. Sincerity is calm. It’s low key. It’s,
JORDAN BELFORT
Video Transcript	19

-- 20 of 40 --

“The only problem you’ll have is I didn’t call you six months
ago and get you started then. 	Sound fair enough?” So that’s
a Language Pattern.
3) 	And that last, “Sound fair enough?” That’s your reasonable
man statement. Not like, “SOUND FAIR ENOUGH!!??”
(angrylike). 	Or, “Sound fair enough?” (nasally), or “Sound
fair enough?” (highpitched). You’re reasonable; I’m
reasonable; it’s no big deal. That’s how you close somebody.
So, it’s not a close of certainty to apply pressure—it’s just
reasonable, “Sound fair enough?” (no big deal).
Language Patterns
So, let me go through the actual Language Patterns themselves, just
to give you an example of a language pattern for an Opening, for the
Body, and for the Close. That will allow you to write your scripts very,
very effectively in not that much time. The goal is, not just for you
to be able to write a GREAT script, but also to save time because
time is money. So, I want to give you some short cuts into writing
effective scripts.
1. 	So, let’s start like this. In the L4"$%$5M1
a. 	When you introduce yourself and say, “How’s it going
today?,” you’re enthusiastic, you sound good, and you get
people keyed into your voice.
b. 	The next pattern I want to talk about is the reason for
your call. Now listen to me very closely at the tone of my
voice. I say, “How you going today?” They say, “Great.”
You say, “Great. Well the reason for the call today is, we’re
reaching out to people in your area,” and include whatever
JORDAN BELFORT
Video Transcript	20

-- 21 of 40 --

it is you’re selling, right? “We’re contacting homeowners in
your area,” or “Contacting people on our list.” So you say,
“The reason for the call today is that we’re reaching out to
people in your area.”
Now remember, there’s got to be a reason why you called
the person. It can’t be, “I just called you out of the blue.” I
didn’t call you yesterday, I didn’t call you tomorrow, I called
you now. So, there’s a reason attached to it. That’s called
I<"1N3/&%#"*. I’m going to write that down. It’s called
>*.#h8+&'4.%6##E(0#.++.(&'-//92#=*.(#9"8#8+.#h8+&'4.%+2#'&#
dramatically increases your response rate so that someone
actually agrees with you or lets you get what you want.
h8+&'4.%+#-%.#5.%92#5.%92#$"=.%)8/6##
So I would say, watch, “Well, the reason for the call is
we’re reaching out to people in your area.” Or, now I can
put a tone to that and say, “Great. Well, the reason for
the call today.” It’s like, I drop my tone a bit. And now
all of a sudden, the reason takes on a much more, “Ooh,
what is it? Is it a secret?”—you create scarcity. You
create urgency.
c. 	Another example of a Language Pattern is when you ask
somebody. So, one of the things that you would do is,
you have certain patterns and they’re all in your workbook
here. Let’s say you’re trying to qualify someone for an
'(5.+&,.(&6##E(02#9"8#=-(&.0#&"#4(0#"8&#*"=#,83*#/'I8'0#
money they have. You wouldn’t say, “How much money
are you liquid for right now?” They’ll say, “Whoa! That’s
a pretty intrusive question.” So you’re not going to get the
response you want. First, just take that Language Pattern
and say, “How much are you liquid for right now? Just a
JORDAN BELFORT
Video Transcript	21

-- 22 of 40 --

ballpark?” And you get this sort of like, “No big deal. Just
for informational purposes. Not a big deal.” It’s called your
Not a Big Deal Tone. It’s just for informational purposes.
You would never say, “So how much do you have in the bank
right now?” You’d say, “What are you liquid for right now?
Just a ball park. No big deal.” And then you’ll probably
get the response versus having someone clam up and
breaking rapport.
2. 	Now let’s move on from the Intro into &<"1@+!(:
a. 	This is a language pattern I mentioned before, which is,
your transition from the Intro to the Main Body, which is,
“Well, let me say this, John. Based on everything you just
2415'&)'A"C'&012'12'5"(81&"7*'4'!"#$"%&'(&'$)#'*)+,''B)=C'#"47'
quick. The name of the program is X.” And that’s how you
start your transition.
b. 	And the Language Patterns that you use in the Main Body
-%.#X8+&#-+#',$"%&-(&6##<#="(@&#1'5.#9"8#+$.3'43#$-&&.%(+#
because they vary so much industry to industry. But the
important thing is that the Main Body should be no more
than a short paragraph. And it should end with, “Make
sense so far? You follow what I’m saying?”
If a pattern in the Main Body is three or four paragraphs,
9"8#='//#/"+.#$."$/.6##B"2#.-3*#7'&.G+'C.0#3*8(;#")#
'()"%,-&'"(#,8+&#7.#7.(.4&G"%'.(&.02#("&#).-&8%.G"%'.(&.06#
And if you end with, “Are you following? Make sense so
far?” And he says, “Yeah.” You say, “Okay. Great.” And
if he says, “Yes,” you don’t keep plowing on. You move
on. And, make sure that your tone, again, is one of
absolute Certainty.
JORDAN BELFORT
Video Transcript	22

-- 23 of 40 --

So if he says, “Yeah, it’s good.” You don’t say, “Great!!!”
like Tony the Tiger. You should say, “Great!” and go in a
little bit above him. What you’re doing is leading him into a
place of absolute certainty.
3. 	Now let’s move from The Main Body of the script into &<"1K'+/":
a. 	One Pattern is, “Now, getting started is very simple. It’s
just a question of some basic information to get the
account set up. And then, we handle everything else for
you.” Now that, depending on what industry you’re in, is a
very, very potent closing mechanism that essentially says,
“It’s no big deal to get started.”
Remember, this is a very important psychological trigger,
is that people are always running this equation: Effort In to
J"8"(&'S+&. How much effort do they have to put in? How
,83*#%'+;#0"#&*.9#*-5.#&"#$8&#'(V##E(0#*"=#,-(9#7.(.4&+#
are they going to get from their effort?
Your Language Pattern must also be congruent with your
tone. In other words, if you say to someone, “Now getting
started is very, very simple” (harshly), they’ll be like, “What
the hell was that?” If you say, “Now getting started is very
simple. Just a question of some basic information to get
the account set up. We handle everything else for you.”
(not a big deal tone), that’s a very powerful pattern for
making it sound so simple for them to get started.
And, by the way, as an actual entrepreneur or sales
person, make sure that the system you have in place is
JORDAN BELFORT
Video Transcript	23

-- 24 of 40 --

simple. You wouldn’t want to say that if it actually was very
complicated. And, be sure that your marketing makes it
very, very easy for your clients to say,“Yes.” It shouldn’t
be that they say, “Yes,” and then have to jump through
45 hoops.
Now, there are some situations where if there’s banking
stuff or mortgages involved, there are some hoops that
need to be jumped through. But, make it clear, “Listen,
we make this very, very simple for you. We handle all the
paperwork. We hold your hand every step of the way.
And it will just take a couple of minutes of your time.” Or
say whatever amount of time is realistic. So that would
be something in your Close. Those are just very basic
examples of Language Patterns. I want to give you just
now, two more Patterns here that you could focus on.
b. 	This is one of the most important things that you want to
do. Let’s say you gave a great presentation but they want
&"#&*'(;#-7"8&#'&6#>*.#4%+&#I8.+&'"(#9"8@%.#1"'(1#&"#-+;#
them after they say, “Let me think about it,” is “Does the
idea actually make sense, then? Do you like the idea?”
That’s the beginning of a Language Pattern where you
would resell. Essentially, you resell the company and
the product.
So, if someone says, “Let me think about it,” we typically
know they’re being held back by one of The 3 Tens. So
the idea here is to raise their levels of certainty. So your
4%+&#J-(18-1.#K-&&.%(#'+#1"'(1#&"#7.2#“Well, let me ask you
a question. Does the idea make sense to you? Do you
like the idea?”
JORDAN BELFORT
Video Transcript	24

-- 25 of 40 --

So, if they say, “Well, I don’t have enough money right
now.” You don’t say, “Well, how come?” Or, “How much
do you have?” You don’t play into the objection. You
0.D.3&#-(0#&*.(#9"8#%8(#-#$-&&.%(#")2#“I hear what you’re
saying, but let me ask you a question. Does the idea
make sense to you? Do you like the idea?”
Now notice the tone there. That was what I call a
MoneyAside Tone . What I’m saying is, “Well, let me ask
you a question. All things being equal, hypothetically,
does the idea make sense to you? Do you like the idea?”
I would never actually say in the Language Pattern, “and
money aside.”
You make the whole thing an academic exercise. And, by
using the tonality that disarms them, you get that across
to them emotionally. And, you still get them to stay in
the sale. I’d say, “Well, let me ask you a question. Does
the idea make sense to you? Do you like the idea?” And
typically they’ll say, “Well, yeah. It sounds pretty good.”
And we know that’s not good enough. We need them to
be absolutely certain that the product is great.
So I would say, “Exactly. Well, let me say this. The true
beauty of the program...”
And then, I would go in and actually start giving them more
7.(.4&+6##O.,.,7.%V##<#+-'02#keep your powder dry. This
'+#=*96##M.3-8+.#-)&.%#9"8#1"#&*%"81*#&*.#4%+&#'('&'-/#M"09#
of your script, they don’t say “Yes” right away. So you
want to have even MORE powerful stuff to help them come
to the right decision, which is the decision to buy your
product. That’s your next Language Pattern.
JORDAN BELFORT
Video Transcript	25

-- 26 of 40 --

c. 	And then the last Pattern is sort of what I call the Rich
Man, Poor Man pattern. And what that really means is,
you say, “What’s the worst that can possibly happen? Let’s
say I’m wrong. And my product is not everything that I say
it is, okay? What’s the worst that can possibly happen?”
You would lay out for them. If it was the stock market,
you’d say, “Listen. The Stock goes down three points and
you lose $1,000. It’s not going to make you a poor man.”
They’ll say, “No.” And you say, “On the upside, even if
I’m right and it does go up 20 points, and you make 3 or
4,000, that’s not going to make you a rich man, either.”
And they’ll say, “No.” You say, “Exactly. What it WILL do, is
serve as a benchmark for future business and show what I
can do for you besides this one transaction.”
This is for stocks, but it applies to anything. Whether
they’re buying, you know, a coaching program or they’re
buying a car. “What’s the worst that can possibly happen?”
Now of course, in some industries, it really works very well
when there are ways to mitigate loss through guarantees, if
there are any stop losses or insurance policies where there
is no equity in the policy. They can cash in.
You have all these Patterns depending on the industry, but
the thing to remember is that you’re trying to speak to a
$.%+"(#=*"#'+#%8(('(1#&*'+#7.(.4&\R))"%&#<(2#M.(.4&#N8&6##
In the overall structure here, it would be like this. The
worst that can happen versus the best. And, if the worst
thing that can happen here is basically nothing because
JORDAN BELFORT
Video Transcript	26

-- 27 of 40 --

&*.%.@+#-#18-%-(&..2#-(0#"(#&*.#8$+'0.2#&*.#7.(.4&+#1"#)-%#
beyond the product itself—it’s got a longterm relationship.
And you start future pacing by saying, “What I could do for
you besides this, over the long term. We work on referrals.
We’re here to be an asset for you and your family for the
longterm,” it has a much, much more profound impact.”
Live Demonstration
So what we’re going to do now is actually cut to the footage of me
teaching script building live to people who are developing a script together
in a company. And you’ll see stepbystep how I use the exact tools and
techniques I just talked about to take a script that was okay and make it
perfect, seamless, and elegant. And, to have it so that no matter what the
skill level is of the actual sales person, they can take that and use it at the
*'1*.+&#/.5./#&"#'(D8.(3.#$."$/.#-(0#3/"+.#.5.%9"(.#=*"#'+#3/"+.-7/.6##
60*%4&,@3%'!%$51O"B+$/&*-&%+$
JB: 	Jordan Belfort
MP: Male Participant
JB: Now, watch as I read this script and you’re going to notice something
here that I myself might stumble a bit on the words. Some of the words
,'1*&#("&#4&#'(#&*.#+$.3'43#(8,7.%#")#+9//-7/.+#'(#.-3*#+.(&.(3.6##>*-&@+#
7.3-8+.#&*'+#'+#&*.#4%+&#0%-)&#")#&*.#3"/0#3-//6##>*'+#'+#1"'(1#&"#.5"/5.#"5.%#
the next two to three days until it’s absolutely perfect—until every single
+9//-7/.#-(0#.5.%9#$-%&#D"=+#$.%).3&/9\+"#&*-&#'&#+"8(0+#/';.#,8+'3_#+"#
that it sounds like poetry when you’re talking on the phone.
What do you do if you’re in the car and you put on the radio, and all of a
sudden a song comes on you don’t like. What do you do? Change the
JORDAN BELFORT
Video Transcript	27

-- 28 of 40 --

station or turn it off, right? That’s what happens if you get on the phone
with somebody and your tonality is slightly off. They tune you out. You
need to sound exactly right. It’s a very short script, so we’re going to go
through this because I’m going to give you one basic shot at it, okay?
And, I’m going to go through it line by line. I’m going to go around the
room and every one of us is going to get this script exactly right, or we’re
not going home tonight. We’ll stay up all night long till 3:00 in the morning
until we get this right. Just kidding.
But we’re going to get the script exactly right. Okay? So, here’s how it
starts. Ready?
JB: “Hi. Is John there?” Let me hear you say it.
MP: “Hi. Is John there?”
JB: Good. “Hey John. Jordan Belfort calling from The Keller Group,
down here in La Jolla, California? How’s it going today?” Let’s hear
you say it.
MP: “Hey John. Jordan Belfort calling…
JB: Don’t worry. Keep going.
MP: “Hey John. Joey calling from The Keller Group, down here in La
Jolla, California. How’s it going today?”
JB: Okay. Let’s hear it again. Start with, “Hey John.”
MP: “Hey John. Joey calling from The Keller Group, down here in La
Jolla, California. How’s it going today?”
JB: Okay. He says, “The Keller Group.” Is it a question or a declarative?
JORDAN BELFORT
Video Transcript	28

-- 29 of 40 --

What sounds better? “Hey, John. Calling from The Keller Group.”
Or, “John, calling from The Keller Group? down here in La Jolla,
California? How’s it going today?”
# 	####A*'3*#'+#7.&&.%V##>*.#4%+&#"%#+.3"(0V##B.3"(06##<&@+#("&6##J'+&.(#&"#
me. This is Science. It sounds better when you raise your voice
up and turn it into a question. Say it. Say it again. Just from Joey.
“Calling from The Keller Group?”
MP: “Joey, calling from The Keller Group? down in La Jolla, California?”
How’s it going today??
JB: Now, when you say, “down here in La Jolla, California,” there’s a
reason why he’d say, “down here in La Jolla, California,” which is not
really correct English. What you’re trying to say here is you’re talking
to the guy in a very familiar, friendly way. I went back and forth with
this one sentence for like 30 minutes. Just so you know how much
attention I pay to a script because every word will increase your
close rate.
I went back and forth from, “down here in La Jolla,” to “over in La
Jolla,” to “in La Jolla, California” because I know that when the right
word is used, it sounds better to the person that’s listening to it.
And, you’ll get to keep listening with more attention, okay? If the
person is in California, chances are, they’re north of here, correct?
Because we’re in Southern California, which is why it says, “down
here in La Jolla, California.” And, that’s meant to say a sort of good
old, “know what, friend?” So you’re not saying, “down here in La
Jolla, California.” It’s like saying, “Hey Jordan, I’m calling from The
Keller Group? down in La Jolla, California? How’s it going today?”
l%'.(0/96##l%'.(0/96##:$7.-&6##J.&@+#*.-%#'&#-1-'(6
JORDAN BELFORT
Video Transcript	29

-- 30 of 40 --

MP: “ Hey John. Joey calling from The Keller Group, down here in La
Jolla, California. How’s it going today?”
JB: Don’t worry about it. Nope. Watch. “Joey calling from The Keller
Group?” Take a question mark and put a question mark after the
word “group.” Write it in the script. After the word “group.” Put a
question mark in there.
MP: “ Joey calling from The Keller Group? I’m here in La Jolla, California.
How’s it going today?”
JB: “I’m okay.”
MP: “Great! Now you might have heard of The Keller Group…”
JB: Stop. Say it again.
MP: Okay, great. “Great! Now you might have heard of The
Keller Group…”
JB” Stop. What sounds better? That or, “Now, you might have heard of
The Keller Group.” Which is better? Second one. You say it.
MP: “You might have heard of The Keller Group.”
JB: Stop. Or, “Now, you might have heard of The Keller Group.” Which
is better? There’s a difference between the two. He says, “Now you
might have heard of The Keller Group.” 	And I say, “Now you might
have heard of The Keller Group.”
There is a different tone in my voice. There’s something that you
have that’s right here—it’s bottled enthusiasm, it’s a power that
9"8#*-5.#&"#'(D8.(3.2#-(0#'&#3",.+#)%",#'(#9"8%#18&+2#"%#=*-&#9"8#
JORDAN BELFORT
Video Transcript	30

-- 31 of 40 --

3-//#9"8%#3*'#=*-&.5.%#'&#,'1*&#7.6#<)#9"8#=-(&#&"#'(D8.(3.2#+-9#&"#
someone, “Let me tell you something here. You have NO idea.
This is the best product since sliced bread.” That’s one way. “Let me
tell you something. You have no idea. This is the best….” (Jordan
drops tone down into a whisper.)
Which is more powerful? The second. And I whisper. The most
powerful thing is a whisper. So, what I’m trying to say is, it’s not just
about saying, “You have no idea.” You have to have certainty. What
sounds different? “The Certainty,” or the “CERtainty.” Is there a
difference in those two words? The “Certainty.” The “CERtainty.” It’s
a power you have. Everybody has to tap into this power that you have,
%'1*&#'(#9"8%#18&#*.%.6##E(02#9"8#*-5.#&"#8+.#&*'+#&"#'(D8.(3.#$."$/.6##
It’s a power that you have and it comes through the phone lines and
creates a mental picture that you’re sharp as a tack, enthusiastic
-+#*.//2#-(0#-(#.F$.%&#'(#9"8%#4./06##A*.(#9"8#+-92#“Now, you might
have heard of The Keller Group…” And I say, “You MIGHT have
heard of The Keller.” (Jordan says with a whisper.)
Which is better? The second one. They’re not in the same league.
I want to say, “Now,” the word NOW. Circle it. Circle the word
now. “Now” is a transition word. NOW. Like, “Well.” These are
two of your basic transition words. “Well, the reason for the call.”
(Jordan drops voice down.) Not, “Well the reason for the call.” “Well,
the reason for the call.” “Now, you might have heard of The Keller
Group.” When you see a word there, like “now” or “now,” typically,
that word is there to create a slight pause, and to transition to
another powerful thought.
See, you’re not just going to read this and say, “Now. You might
have heard of The Keller Group. We’re one of the fastest growing
JORDAN BELFORT
Video Transcript	31

-- 32 of 40 --

customer getting companies in America.” You sound like a idiot
if you do that. Say to the guy, “Now, you might have heard of The
Keller Group.” There’s an urgency in my tone. Like this guy is
thinking, “What?!!!!?? Should I get it? What is it?” 	The tone will
make an impact.
Every two or three words you say with a tone attached, there are two
or three unspoken words that he’s hearing unconsciously. So, when
I say to someone, “Jordan, Calling from The Keller Group?” what
words are there? “Right? Right? You’ve heard of us, right?” This
is the unspoken word that comes. And when I phrase something as
a question,” I say, “The Keller Group?” This is, by the way, you can
see these a thousand times in the script, especially in the close.
“The Keller Group?” with a question at the end? The word that
comes after that is “Right? Right?”
MP: It makes them want to know.
JB: Bingo. They want to know because they don’t want to feel out of the
loop. Everybody has a tremendous, innate desire to feel like they’re
quick on the uptake. So, if you say to the guy, “The Keller Group?”
You wouldn’t say, “Right? You’ve heard of us. Right?” 	If you say
it, he’s like, “what are you talking about?” You don’t have time for
those words. The script will become bulky and not work. It would
not be workable. By simply phrasing something as a question, you
-00.0#"(#)"8%#"%#45.#="%0+#&*-&#+&-%&.0#&*.#189#&*'(;'(1#*.@+#'(#+9(3#
with you.
This is where rapport is developed. That’s what it’s about. Tonal
patterns that get the guy in sync with you. You get rapport going
right to get him viewing you as those things, right in the beginning.
JORDAN BELFORT
Video Transcript	32

-- 33 of 40 --

Now, later on, in the second part as we qualify him, you’re going
to learn how to get the person to feel that, A, you’re just like him,
likeability, the same kind of people and B, that you care, you learn to
get the caring tone, and how to sound like a reasonable man. There
-%.#-//#&*.+.#$"=.%)8/#&"(.+6##>*'+#'+#X8+&#&*.#4%+&#"(.2#-(0#'&@+#-#5.%9#
powerful one. That’s what it does.
The question infers what’s called a microagreement. So you might
4(0#+",.&',.+#9"8@%.#1"'(1#&"#1'5.#&*%..#+&-&.,.(&+6#E(0#9"8#%-'+.#
your voice again, again, again. And what you’re say is, “Right?
Right? Right?”
My daughter, when she was little, she was the best salesperson.
She’d say. “Dad, we’re going to the toy store, right? You said so,
right?” You ever hear a kid do that—they naturally know how to do
this. They’ll phrase something. It’s like, “You said you were going to
the store, right? Right?” “What? Did I? No.”
“You told me you’d take me to the bigger store, right?” When they
say this, you start thinking, “I don’t know. Did I?” You search your
memory. That’s all you’re doing. 	Right here. Okay? So let’s keep
going now. Start from the beginning, “Hi is Jordan there.”
MP: “Hi, is Jordan there.”
JB: Say it again.
MP: “Hi, is Jordan there.”
JB: “HI! Is Jordan there.”
MP: “Hi, is Jordan there.”
JB: “Right here.”

MP: “Hey Jordan. Joey from the The Keller Group? Down here in La
Jolla, California? How’s it going today?””
JB: Okay. Let’s hear it again. Ready? “Hi, is Joey there?” Let’s go
back. I’ll do it too. “Hey, is Joe there? Hey Joe. Jordan Belfort
calling from The Keller Group? In La Jolla California? How’s it
going today?”
MP: “Good.”
JB: Cross off the words “down here.” Cross off “down here.” There we
go. The revision’s are beginning. Ready? Begin. And put the word
“over.”#A-&3*#*"=#'&#D"=+#7.&&.%#("=#='&*#&*.#="%02#“over,” versus
“down here.” Too many syllables. Watch.
“Hey, is Joe there? Hey Joe. Jordan Belfort calling from The
Keller Group? Over in La Jolla, California. How’s it going today?”
Smoother. Just with one change. You say, “Okay, great.”
“Now, you might have heard of The Keller Group, Joe. We’re one of
the fastest growing customergetting companies in America, working
with plumbers and electricians like yourself to get new customers for
your business.”
“Right now, we have about 55 new customers a week. Right here
in San Clemente. We’re in San Clemente now. San Clemente. And
they’re all looking for a plumber. Would you like me to have them
call you?”
That’s your basic script, said really, really, poorly. Because there
were some things that didn’t make sense to me. Let’s try it again.
Say it to me. “Hi. Is Jordan there?”

MP: “Hi. Is Jordan there?”
JB: “Right here.”
MP: “Hey, Jordan. Joey calling from The Keller Group? Over here in La
Jolla, California? How’s it going today?”
JB: “I’m okay.”
MP: “Okay, great. Now, you might have heard of The Keller Group.
We’re one of the fastest growing customer getting companies in
America, working with plumbers and electricians like yourself to get
you new customers for your business. Right now, we have about 55
new customers a week. Right here in San Clemente. And they’re all
looking for a plumber. Would you like me to have them call you?”

MP: “This is him.”
JB: “Hey Joe. Jordan Belfort calling from The Keller Group, over in La
Jolla, California? How’s it going today?”

-- 36 of 40 --

MP: “Good.”
JB: “Great. Now you might have heard of The Keller Group. We’re
one of the fastest growing customer getting companies in America,
working with plumbers and electricians.” Cross out electricians.
“We’re one of the fastest growing customer getting companies
in America, working with plumbers like yourself to get you new
customers for your business. Now right now.” Add the word “now.”
Notice you see the word transition? The transition? Now before
right now. Reason is, it allows you to pause and change your tonality
again. “Now. Right now.” Okay?
“Now. Right now. We have about 55 new customers a week, right
here in San Clemente. And they’re all looking for a plumber. Would
you be interested in speaking to them? Would you like us to have
them call you?”

might have heard of The Keller Group.”

Video Transcript	38

-- 39 of 40 --

MP: “Now you might have heard of The Keller Group. We’re one of the
fastest growing customer getting companies in America—working
with plumbers, like yourself, to bring you new customers for your
business. Now, right now, we have about 55 new customers a week
in San Clemente, who are all looking for a plumber. Would you like
us to have them call you?”
JB: Good. Give him a round of applause here.
So, again, thanks very much. Be sure to use this DVD with your manual.
If you’re listening to this on CD again, you can listen to it in the car. But
always refer back to the manual so that you can really take this as a
cohesive package. And make sure that your company, or if it’s just you as
a sales person yourself, that you can take these scripts and go down The
Straight Line in a way that allows you to really get exactly what you want.
And, that is to close the people who should be closed and to build your
business on solid footing so you can soar, both for the shortterm and the
longterm.
Thanks, and see you soon.
JORDAN BELFORT
Video Transcript	39

-- 40 of 40 --
